title: When Monkeys Enter the Uncanny Valley
gist: A new PLOS Biology study finds that macaques, like humans, experience an 'uncanny valley' response to increasingly humanlike avatars; German and Belgian researchers built lifelike 3-D macaque avatars using deep-learning software trained on 42 tracked body key points from eight camera angles.

insights.claim: The 'uncanny valley' effect — coined by Japanese roboticist Masahiro Mori, where an avatar becomes familiar as it grows more humanlike but eventually triggers feelings of creepiness (as with humanoid robots like China's 'U1s') — is not unique to humans: a new study in PLOS Biology finds other primates also experience it.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Evidence that uncanny-valley aversion is a deeper, cross-species perceptual phenomenon rather than a human-specific cultural response, relevant to robotics/avatar design research and human-computer-interaction product design.
insights.locator: Interestingly, a new study published in PLOS Biology

insights.claim: A team of brain and intelligent systems researchers in Germany and Belgium built lifelike, full-body 3-D macaque avatars to study neural mechanisms of social communication, choosing macaques (Macaca mulatta) for their highly expressive communication.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Establishes a new experimental tool (controllable primate avatars) for probing social-cognition neuroscience that could generalize to human social-perception research.
insights.locator: A team of brain and intelligent systems researchers

insights.claim: Because acquiring movement data from markers affixed to real monkeys was impractical, researchers instead tracked 42 key points (elbow, wrist, tail-base, etc.) from eight camera angles and processed the data with deep-learning software to build the avatars.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A concrete markerless motion-capture + deep-learning pipeline (42 keypoints, 8 cameras) that could be adapted for other animal-behavior or avatar-generation research applications.
insights.locator: Because it was impractical to acquire enough movement data
